content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The site was handed to me as “already hardened.” Login URL changed, a security plugin running, a two-factor prompt on the way in. The person who built it was proud of it, and by the usual checklist it looked fine.
So I did the thing I do before I trust any checklist. I opened a browser and typed the site’s address followed by /wp-config.php.save.
It loaded. Not a blank page, not a 404. The raw contents of a text file, served straight to my browser, starting with the database name and moving on to the database user, the database password, and eight authentication keys. An editor had left a .save copy behind during some long-ago fix, and the server, having no reason to run a file with a .save extension through PHP, did the only thing it knew how to do. It printed it.
Every control on that site was now theatre. It did not matter that the login URL had moved or that two-factor was on. With the database password in hand, an attacker does not visit the login page. They connect to the database directly, or they forge a valid session cookie from the leaked salts and walk in as an administrator. One forgotten file had quietly voided every other layer.
Why this one file is the highest-value target on the server
wp-config.php is the first thing WordPress reads on every request, before it loads anything else. It holds four database constants (name, user, password, host), plus eight secret keys and salts that sign your login cookies, plus the table prefix. Everything a site needs to reach its database and prove who is logged in lives in that one file, in plain readable text.
Here is the part that makes it a mass-scale problem rather than a your-site problem. WordPress runs roughly 43% of all websites (W3Techs, ongoing). The file has the same name, the same default location, and the same internal structure across every one of those installs. A script does not need to understand your site to know that /wp-config.php is where the credentials sit. It is the same address on millions of doors.
And credentials are what the attackers are actually after. According to the Verizon 2025 DBIR, 88% of attacks against basic web applications involved stolen credentials. Read a site’s config file and you have skipped the entire theft: you are not stealing credentials, you are being handed them. No login attempt, no lockout to trip, nothing for a brute-force counter to notice. This is why I have started telling people that the login page is the noisy front door and wp-config.php is the unlocked window nobody photographs.
Nobody types the password. They get the file to give it up.
When I audit a site now, I stop treating this as a password problem and start treating it as a file problem. There are four ways that file gets read, and none of them require knowing a single thing about your site in advance.
The first is a vulnerable plugin with a file-read flaw, either Local File Inclusion or path traversal (CWE-22). A request stuffed with ../../../wp-config.php climbs out of the directory a plugin was supposed to stay inside and reads a file it was never meant to touch. This is not an edge case. According to Patchstack's State of WordPress Security in 2026, 11,334 new vulnerabilities were disclosed across the WordPress ecosystem in 2025, up 42% year over year, and 91% of them lived in plugins rather than core. Every one of those plugins runs with permission to read wp-config.php.
The second is a “files accessible to external parties” flaw (CWE-552), where a plugin or a sloppy endpoint serves a private file to anyone who asks. The third is source disclosure: if PHP stops executing for a moment during a bad deploy, a direct request to /wp-config.php returns the raw code as text, credentials and all. The fourth is the one that got the site I opened this story with, and the one that catches experienced people most often. A backup copy. wp-config.php.bak, wp-config.php.save, wp-config.php~, wp-config.old, wp-config.php.txt. The server serves those as plaintext because it has no reason to interpret them.
The reason this bites harder in 2026 than it did a few years ago is speed. Patchstack’s 2026 data shows 20% of heavily exploited vulnerabilities are hit within six hours of disclosure, 45% within a day, and 70% within a week. Sophos and Hostinger reporting puts the raw volume at roughly 13,000 WordPress sites compromised per day. The gap between “a plugin flaw goes public” and “a bot is using it to read your config” is now measured in hours, not months. You are not racing a person. You are racing a schedule.
The fix is a sequence, and the order is the whole lesson
The instinct, once you understand the threat, is to reach for the most dramatic change first. Resist it. The goal is not to make the file harder to guess. Its location is public knowledge and always will be. The goal is that when something requests it, the request fails before it can read a byte.
Here is the order I apply, lowest risk first, because I learned the hard way that the order matters more than any single step. Deny direct access to the file at the server level, so a request for wp-config.php returns 404 regardless of where the file sits. In the same rule, reject the backup extensions: .bak, .save, .old, .txt, and the tilde variant. Set permissions to 600 so only the owner can read it, which on shared hosting is what stops the account next door from reading your credentials. Where the host layout genuinely allows it, move the file one directory above the web root, where a direct HTTP request has nothing to hit. Turn off the dashboard file editor with DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT. Make sure debug output is off in production so debug.log is not quietly leaking queries and paths. And rotate the keys and salts, which invalidates every active session and is exactly what you want after any exposure, even a brief one.
The mistake that fixed this order in my head happened years ago on a live client site at the end of a long day. I went for the highest-impact change first, hand-editing rewrite rules, and put a syntax error in .htaccess that returned a 500 across the whole site. The blast radius and the payoff live in the same edit. So now the near-zero-risk reductions go first, each one verified, and the risky moves go last with a rollback already staged. Reduction you are afraid to apply is reduction you never finish.
One number keeps me honest about not stopping at the firewall. Patchstack’s 2026 pentest of common defences (internal WAFs, Cloudflare, Imunify360, ModSecurity) found they blocked only 12% of attacks against known-exploited vulnerabilities in one test and 26% in a broader one, which lines up with the older Hostinger and Sophos figure that 87.8% of exploit attempts bypass standard hosting firewalls. A rule that rejects the request for the file itself, at your own server’s rewrite layer, does not depend on an upstream filter recognizing the payload. It is a different layer, not a substitute for one.
I will name the tools here, because pretending I maintain this by hand across a portfolio would be dishonest. We run WordPress security across our properties at Squirrly, and after enough hand-edited .htaccess files drifted out of sync between sites, we standardized the common-file rules with WP Ghost, which returns 404 for wp-config.php and its siblings at the rewrite layer and covers the backup-extension set I would otherwise have to remember to enumerate, and kept Wordfence for scanning. That split is deliberate. WP Ghost is a prevention layer; it is not a scanner. If a config file was already read and something was planted, the honest first move is Wordfence or MalCare to find and clean it, then reduce the surface to keep it clean. Prevention hardens a clean site. It does not disinfect a compromised one. Strip both names out of that paragraph and the sequence still stands, which is the test I hold every recommendation to.
Why a 404 is not obscurity
Someone always raises the objection, so let me answer it before you do: isn’t returning 404 for a file everyone knows exists just security through obscurity? No, and the distinction is mechanical, not rhetorical. Obscurity means the resource is still there and you are betting nobody looks hard enough. A rejection at the rewrite layer means the request for the file never reaches code that can read it. The file can sit exactly where it always did; the difference is that the answer to “may I read it” is now no, delivered before PHP loads. You did not hide the key. You deleted the door the key was behind.
The .save file that started this story took about four seconds to fix once I saw it: delete the copy, add a server rule that rejects the whole family of backup extensions, rotate the keys because the credentials had been sitting in public for who knows how long, and confirm the same request now returns 404. The site had spent real effort on the visible controls and left the one file that overrides all of them sitting in the open with a helpful extra copy beside it.
If you run WordPress, do not take my word for your own site. Open a browser, type your domain, and request /wp-config.php.bak, then .save, then ~. If any of them return anything but a 404, you have found your afternoon. Fix that one file first, in order, with the keys rotated at the end. The password on your login screen is the lock everyone photographs. This is the window nobody does.
